title: Cat Licked Permethrin Cream: Emergency Triage and Veterinary Advice
meta_desc: Permethrin is highly toxic to cats. If your cat licked permethrin cream, this is a life-threatening emergency. Contact a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Permethrin is highly toxic to cats and requires immediate emergency veterinary intervention. If your cat has been exposed to permethrin-based products, contact a veterinarian or pet poison control immediately as symptoms can rapidly escalate to life-threatening seizures.

What is permethrin toxicity and why is it dangerous for cats?

Permethrin is a synthetic insecticide commonly found in human scabies treatments, insect repellents, and dog flea medications. While generally safe for humans and dogs, cats lack the specific liver enzyme (glucuronosyltransferase) required to break down this chemical. When a cat licks even a small amount of permethrin cream from their owner's skin, it can lead to a rapid and dangerous buildup in their system, affecting their central nervous system.

Is permethrin exposure a veterinary emergency for my cat?

  • Yes. This is a high-priority medical emergency that is potentially fatal if not treated immediately.
  • Symptoms such as drooling, ear twitching, skin flicking, and tremors can escalate quickly into full-body seizures and hyperthermia.
  • Do not wait for symptoms to manifest; if you suspect ingestion or exposure, contact an emergency veterinarian or a pet poison control hotline right away.

Why should I take a photo of the product packaging for my veterinarian?

  • If it is safe to do so, take a clear photo of the product packaging, specifically the ingredient list and the concentration percentage.
  • Providing this photo allows the veterinarian or poison control specialist to determine the exact dosage your cat may have been exposed to.
  • This information is vital for the medical team to prepare specific treatments, such as intravenous lipid emulsions or muscle relaxants, before you arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If a cat licks permethrin cream, initial assessment of the respiratory and cardiovascular systems is important. The animal should be bathed with a mild detergent and cool water, gently washing the area to avoid stimulating circulation and enhancing skin absorption. Activated charcoal (2-8 g/kg) may be administered, followed by a saline cathartic (magnesium or sodium sulfate solution at 0.5 mg/kg), while vegetable oils and fats should be avoided as they promote intestinal absorption of pyrethrins. Symptomatic and supportive care is generally required. Seizures can be controlled with diazepam (0.2-2 mg/kg, IV to effect) or methocarbamol (55-220 mg/kg, IV, not exceeding 200 mg/min). If these are too short-acting, phenobarbital or pentobarbital (IV to effect) may be used. IV fluids should be given as needed, and IV lipid emulsion solution may be beneficial.

Chapter: Toxicology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 3064)

faqs.question: What are the first signs of permethrin poisoning in cats?
faqs.answer: Initial signs often include excessive drooling, agitation, and localized muscle twitches, particularly around the ears and face, which can progress to severe tremors.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat anything at home to stop the poisoning?
faqs.answer: No. Never attempt to induce vomiting or give home remedies to a cat that has ingested permethrin. Only a veterinarian can safely provide the necessary supportive care and medications.

faqs.question: How long does it take for symptoms to appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically appear within 1 to 6 hours of exposure, but in some cases, they may be delayed for up to 24 hours. Immediate veterinary consultation is required regardless of symptom status.
